ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for white rice with two vegetable side dishes as a composite Singaporean hawker dish; closest match is cooked rice food product (FOODON_00002694).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice dishes broadly; no specific code exists for white rice with two vegetable accompaniments as a composite hawker meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite Singaporean hawker rice dish.

allergens: soy (possible, from soy sauce in vegetable preparation)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of white rice with two vegetable dishes typically weighs approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 100 g of two stir-fried or braised vegetable portions.

nutrient_source.title: White Rice With 2 Veg — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'White Rice With 2 Veg' (cr_id G00010633). All per-100g macros, micronutrients, and minerals are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is based on white rice (polished, cooked) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The composite dish GI is dominated by the white rice component (approximately 65–70% of dish weight). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (60.5 g for 300 g serving) / 100 = 43.3. Available carbohydrate = carbohydrate_g (25.19) × serving_g (300) / 100 = 75.57 g total carbohydrate; dietary fibre subtracted: 75.57 − 11.1 = 64.47 g; GL = 72 × 60.5 / 100 ≈ 43.3. Vegetable components have negligible GI impact at typical serving proportions.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g (borderline range), partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or salt in vegetable preparation and choose potassium-rich vegetables such as kangkong, spinach, or sweet potato leaves to maximise the blood-pressure benefit of the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice GI approximately 72 (high GI); 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic load per standard serving approximately 43
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice, red rice, or mixed-grain rice to lower the glycaemic index significantly, and increase the vegetable-to-rice ratio or add a lean protein component to moderate post-meal blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.8 g/100 g (low); sodium 231 mg/100 g; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g and potassium 327 mg/100 g are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content and meaningful dietary fibre and potassium from the vegetable components are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the moderate sodium level warrants portion control and low-sodium cooking methods to keep total daily sodium within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g, potassium, calcium, and antioxidants from leafy green vegetables; low saturated fat; no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Choose anti-inflammatory vegetables such as leafy greens, broccoli, or carrots and avoid heavy oil or oyster sauce in vegetable preparation to keep saturated fat low and support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Rice with 2 Vegetables (白饭配两种蔬菜 / Nasi Putih 2 Sayur) is a common Singaporean hawker-centre and economy rice (cai fan) meal consisting of a portion of steamed white jasmine rice served alongside two portions of cooked vegetables. The vegetables are typically stir-fried or braised leafy greens such as kangkong (water spinach), chye sim (choy sum), kai lan (Chinese broccoli), or long beans, often seasoned with garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, or sambal. The dish is a staple of the economy rice tradition found across Singapore's hawker centres, coffee shops, and food courts, and is popular among all ethnic communities as an affordable, everyday lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 25.2 g carbohydrate, 6.5 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and a notable 3.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g — the fibre and micronutrients (potassium 327 mg, calcium 54 mg, iron 2.55 mg, magnesium 40 mg) largely contributed by the vegetable components. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, reflecting typical hawker seasoning. The dish has a high glycaemic index (approximately 72) driven by the white rice base, making it a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes; substituting brown or mixed-grain rice is a practical modification. The vegetable components contribute meaningful dietary fibre and potassium, which are beneficial for blood pressure management and cardiovascular health. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, though soy sauce in vegetable preparation introduces a potential gluten and soy allergen.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010633) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical provenance.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite hawker dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010633. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no trans fat expected in a dish of steamed rice and stir-fried vegetables with no hydrogenated oils; confirmed negligible. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as confirmed by HPB source — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol. saturated_fat_g of 0.8 g/100 g is estimated from the cooking oil component (approximately 2–3 g oil per 100 g dish at typical hawker preparation); HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (polished, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI is dominated by the white rice component. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 300 g standard hawker serving.
